name: eve_industry_calculator
summary: Calculator for EVE Online industry that explodes T2/PI/reaction recipes down to base materials, with hangar-aware netting.
description:
Helps calculate what materials are needed to produce advanced items in EVE Online (mainly Tech 2 items, but also covers the PI and moon-goo reaction chains that feed into them). T2 blueprints require many different material types across several tiers: base minerals (e.g. Tritanium, Morphite) are used directly in single-step manufacturing (e.g. Acolyte I drones, R.A.M. Robotics); Planetary Industry products form a P1 -> P2 -> P3 chain (each P2 made from 2 P1 basic materials, each P3 made from 2-3 P2 products, e.g. Robotics <- Consumer Electronics + Mechanical Parts); and moon-goo reaction chains produce things like Laser Focusing Crystals through their own multi-step batch reactions (e.g. Tungsten Carbide batches of 10000 <- Sulfuric Acid + Rolled Tungsten Alloy + Nitrogen Fuel Blocks, each of those built from further reactions). The app has three core use cases: (1) enter/edit recipes so they persist in the backend (recipes are mostly static reference data), (2) pick a recipe and a quantity to produce and get the fully recursive list of base materials required, first as a flat total and later as a step-by-step build plan showing what to craft at each tier, and (3) copy in the contents of your hangar so the calculator nets off materials/intermediates you already have at every tier (e.g. if you need 10 Robotics but have 4, it only asks for the Reactive Metals/Consumer Electronics needed for the remaining 6, and lets you reuse partially-built intermediates from earlier steps).

Architecture: 4 Docker containers - Angular frontend (most business logic: BOM explosion, netting math lives here), PHP backend (kept small, mainly persistence/API for recipes and hangar data; framework TBD), MySQL database, and Adminer for DB inspection/administration. Deeper details (DB schema, API shape, recipe data model) are to be worked out when the relevant goals are tackled.

key_decisions.decision: 4-container Docker setup: Angular frontend, PHP backend, MySQL database, Adminer.
key_decisions.rationale: Keeps concerns separated and gives easy DB inspection via Adminer without building custom admin tooling.

key_decisions.decision: Most business logic (BOM explosion, netting calculations) lives in the Angular frontend; the PHP backend stays small and is mainly responsible for persistence/API of recipes and hangar data.
key_decisions.rationale: User's stated preference - backend framework choice is deliberately left open/TBD since the backend's scope is intentionally minimal.

key_decisions.decision: Exclude files/directories not relevant to the project itself via .gitignore - IDE metadata (e.g. PHPStorm's .idea/) and third-party libraries/dependency directories (e.g. vendor/, node_modules/).
key_decisions.rationale: Keeps the repository focused on project source; machine/user-specific and reproducible/installable files shouldn't be tracked.

key_decisions.decision: PHP backend uses Slim Framework (routing + PSR-7 request/response, JSON in/out) with PDO for MySQL persistence, rather than plain PHP or a full-stack framework like Symfony/Laravel.
key_decisions.rationale: User is familiar with Symfony but wanted something lighter for a pure JSON API with no bundles/DI container/scaffolding - fits the existing decision to keep the backend intentionally minimal.

key_decisions.decision: Source lives in two top-level directories: frontend/ (Angular app) and backend/ (PHP app), not nested under an apps/ folder.
key_decisions.rationale: Simple two-app monorepo layout; decided during docker_setup since both Dockerfiles and docker-compose.yml reference these paths directly.

key_decisions.decision: Docker commands (docker run, docker compose ...) must be prefixed with MSYS_NO_PATHCONV=1 when run from Git Bash on Windows.
key_decisions.rationale: Git Bash rewrites POSIX-looking paths (e.g. /app used as a container path or working directory) into Windows paths before they reach Docker, causing errors like "the working directory 'C:/Program Files/Git/app' is invalid". This will recur on any future docker command run from this shell.
